title: Lemon drizzle cake
subtitle: Sharp lemon curd soaked into a warm loaf
excerpt: A butter-soft loaf cake with a sharp lemon crust formed by pouring a sugar syrup over the hot cake straight from the oven. Straightforward to make and reliably good.
sourceNotes: Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg — the basic pound-cake method: equal weights of butter, sugar, eggs, and flour. The lemon drizzle finish is a British mid-twentieth-century refinement of the method, moving from the iced finish to the soaked one for a sharper result.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze undrizzled. Wrap in baking paper and foil. Drizzle after defrosting.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps well for three days in an airtight tin. The crust improves overnight.

glossaryTerms.definition: Beating butter and sugar together until pale and increased in volume. The process incorporates air that expands during baking, giving the cake its lift.

content.text: The drizzle is the thing. A sharp sugar syrup (the juice of two lemons with enough caster sugar to take the raw edge off without blunting the acid) goes over the hot cake the moment it comes out of the oven. The cake absorbs it while it cools, and the surface dries to a crystalline crust. The whole operation takes about 30 seconds. It is more effective than any icing.
This is a loaf cake, not a sandwich cake. The shape matters: the narrow, deep tin gives a thick crumb that holds the syrup rather than letting it run off the edges. Use a 900 g (2 lb) loaf tin.
Heat the oven to 180°C fan. Grease the loaf tin and line it with baking paper, letting the paper overhang the long sides. This makes the finished cake easy to lift out.

: beat the {{unsalted-butter}} and {{caster-sugar}} (the 175 g for the cake) together until the mixture is pale, noticeably increased in volume, and no longer gritty when you rub a little between your fingers. By hand this takes 5–7 minutes of vigorous beating; with an electric hand mixer, 3 minutes on medium speed. Add the lemon zest and beat it in.

Add the {{free-range-eggs}} one at a time, beating well between each addition. If the batter curdles, beat in a tablespoon of flour. Sift in the {{plain-flour}} and {{baking-powder}}, then fold in gently until just combined. Add the {{whole-milk}} and fold through — it loosens the batter enough to drop slowly from the spoon.

Spoon the batter into the tin and smooth the top. Bake for 45–50 minutes. The cake is done when a skewer inserted into the centre comes out clean, the top is golden, and the cake has begun to pull from the tin sides. Domestic ovens drift — start checking at 42 minutes.

While the cake bakes, stir the lemon juice and the 75 g of {{caster-sugar}} (for the drizzle) together until the sugar is mostly dissolved — it does not need to be entirely clear. Take the cake out of the oven, leave it in the tin, and poke it all over with a skewer to a depth of about 3 cm. Pour the drizzle slowly over the hot cake, letting it sink in between pokes. All of it goes on.

Leave the cake in the tin for 10 minutes, then lift it out by the paper and transfer to a rack to cool completely. The surface will dry to a sandy, slightly crystalline finish. Slice only when completely cold.
